prompt:
Estás en un directorio que contiene una tarea de programación. Lee los archivos disponibles y resuelve la tarea de la forma más completa posible.

Puedes crear, editar o eliminar archivos dentro de este directorio. Si el enunciado no especifica un lenguaje, framework o estructura de proyecto, elige una alternativa pragmática.

Al terminar, deja instrucciones claras para ejecutar o revisar tu solución.

No hagas preguntas de seguimiento. Adopta supuestos razonables y documéntalos.

turns.toolResult:
#lang play

#|
Nombre: 
¿Utilizó Whiteboard Policy? (SI o NO):
En caso afirmativo, ¿con quién?:
¿en qué ejercicio(s)?:
|#


;;------------ ;;
;;==== P1 ==== ;;
;;------------ ;;

#| Parte A |#

#|
<s-type> ::= 'Number
           | 'Boolean
           | (list -> <s-type> <s-type>)
|#

#|
<type> ::= (numT)
         | (boolT)
         | (arrowT <type> <type>)
|#
(deftype Type
  (numT)
  (boolT)
  (arrowT arg ret))

;; parse-type :: <s-type> -> Type


#| Parte B |#

#|
<s-expr> ::= <number>
           | true
           | false
           | <symbol>
           | (list + <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (list * <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (list < <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (list if <s-expr> <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | ...
|#

#|
<expr> ::= (num <number>)
         | (tt)
         | (ff)
         | (id <symbol>)
         | (add <expr> <expr>)
         | (mul <expr> <expr>)
         | (lt <expr> <expr>)
         | (ite <expr> <expr> <expr>)
         | ...
|#
(deftype Expr
  (num n)
  (tt)
  (ff)
  (id x)
  (add left right)
  (mul left right)
  (lt left right)
  (ite cond true false)
  '...)


#| Parte C |#

;; parse :: <s-expr> -> Expr


#| Parte D |#

#| TypeEnv Utils |#
(deftype TypeEnv
  (mtTenv)
  (xTenv id type env))

(define empty-tenv (mtTenv))

(define extend-tenv xTenv)

(define (tenv-lookup x env)
  (match env
    [(mtTenv) (error 'tenv-lookup "free identifier: ~a" x)]
    [(xTenv id type rest) (if (symbol=? x id) type (tenv-lookup x rest))]))

;; check-type :: Expr TypeEnv -> Type


#| Parte E |#

#| Language Values |#
;; numV y boolV son wrappers de los valores de Racket.
(deftype Value
  (numV n)
  (boolV v)
  (closureV arg body env))

#| Env Utils |#
;; Recuerde que los ambientes guardan valores, no expresiones.
(deftype Env
  (mtEnv)
  (xtEnv id val env))

(define empty-env (mtEnv))

(define extend-env xtEnv)

(define (lookup x env)
  (match env
    [(mtEnv) (error 'lookup "free identifier: ~a" x)]
    [(xtEnv id val rest) (if (symbol=? x id) val (lookup x rest))]))

;; interp :: Expr Env -> Value


#| Parte F |#

;; run :: <s-expr> -> Value


;;------------ ;;
;;==== P2 ==== ;;
;;------------ ;;

#| Parte A y E |#

#|
<prog> ::= (numbr <number>)
         | (variable <symbol>)
         | (plus <prog> <prog>)
         | (nil)
         | (conz <prog> <prog>)
         | ...
|#
(deftype Prog
  (numbr n)
  (plus l r)
  (nil)
  (conz a b)
  (variable x))

#|
<s-expr> ::= <number>
           | <symbol>
           | (list + <s-prog> <s-prog>)
           | (list nil)
           | (list cons <s-prog> <s-prog>)
           | (list list <s-prog> ...)
           | ...
|#

;; parser :: <s-prog> -> Prog


#| Parte B |#

#|
<pattern> ::= (numP <number>)
            | (varP <symbol>)
            | (nilP)
            | (conzP <pattern> <pattern>)
|#
(deftype Pattern
  (numP n)
  (varP x)
  (nilP)
  (conzP a b))

;; parse-pattern :: <s-prog> -> Pattern


#| Parte C |#

#|
<pvalue> ::= (numerical <number>)
          | (nilV)
          | (conzV <value> <value>)
|#
(deftype PValue
  (numerical n)
  (nilV)
  (conzV a b))

#|
<result> e v ::= (failure e)
               | (success v)
|#
(deftype Result
  (failure e)
  (success v))

;; generate-subst :: Pattern PValue -> (Result String (Listof (Symbol * PValue)))


#| Parte D y E |#

;; num+ PValue PValue -> PValue
(define (num+ v1 v2)
  (match v1
    [(numerical n) (match v2
                [(numerical m) (numerical (+ n m))]
                [_ (error "TypeError: expected a number")])]
    [_ (error "TypeError: expected a number")]))

;; reduce :: Prog (Listof (Symbol * PValue)) -> PValue


#| Parte F |#

#|
Respuesta:
|#

# CC4101 Tarea 3

## Typechecking y Pattern Matching

Para la resolución de la tarea recuerde que

                   Toda función debe estar acompañada de su firma, una breve descripción coloquial (en T3.rkt) y un conjunto significativo de tests (en test.rkt).
                   Todo datatype definido por el usuario (via deftype) debe estar acompañado de
                   una breve descripción coloquial y de la gramática BNF que lo genera.

              Si la función o el datatype no cumple con estas reglas, será ignorado.

         Ejercicio 1                                                                             30 Pt

                (a) [2 Pt] En el archivo T3.rkt se encuentra la definición del tipo de datos Type, que
                    representa los tipos válidos para un lenguaje con valores numéricos, booleanos
                    y funciones de 1°clase (por simplicidad de un solo argumento). Siguiendo los
                    ejemplos, defina la función recursiva
                    ;; parse-type ::   <s-type> -> Type

                    >>> (parse-type 'Number)
                    (numT)
                    >>> (parse-type 'Boolean)
                    (boolT)
                    >>> (parse-type '(-> Number Boolean))
                    (arrowT (numT) (boolT))
                    >>> (parse-type 'String)
                    parse-type: invalid type String

                (b) [4 Pt] En el mismo archivo, se encuentra la definición base de un lenguaje con
                    valores numéricos y booleanos. Extienda la gramática BNF del lenguaje y el
                    tipo de datos Expr para que este soporte la definición y aplicación de funciones
                    de 1°clase. Considere que como se quiere implementar typechecking, al definir
                    funciones se debe indicar explícitamente el tipo del argumento. Puede guiarse
                    con los siguientes ejemplos para la sintaxis concreta y abstracta (aún no necesita
                    definir la función parse):
                    >>> (parse '(fun x Number (+ x 1)))
                    (fun 'x (numT) (add (id 'x) (num 1)))
                    >>> (parse '((fun x Number (* x 2)) 10))
                    (app (fun 'x (numT) (mul (id 'x) (num 2))) (num 10))

(c) [7 Pt] Escriba la función
    ;; parse ::   <s-expr> -> Expr
    que transforma un programa en sintaxis concreta a uno en sintaxis abstracta.

(d) [7 Pt] Implemente la función
    ;; check-type ::   Expr TypeEnv -> Type
    que realiza la comprobación de tipos (typechecking) sobre un programa en sintaxis
    abstracta. Para ello, considere las siguientes reglas:

        Las constantes numéricas son de tipo numérico (numT).

        Las constantes booleanas ( tt y ff) son de tipo boolean (boolT).

        En el caso de la suma y la multiplicación, estas tienen tipo numérico
        (numT), si y solo si, ambos operandos tienen tipo numérico (numT). Por
        otro lado, la operación “menor que” es de tipo boolean (boolT) si ambos
        operandos son de tipo numérico.

        Un identificador obtiene su tipo a partir de la extracción de un ambiente de
        tipos (TypeEnv).

        En el caso de los condicionales (if ), la condición debe tener tipo boolT.
        Por otro lado, ambas ramas de ejecución deben tener el mismo tipo entre sí,
        siendo este el tipo que recibe el condicional completo.

        Las funciones tienen su propio tipo (arrowT) que indica el tipo del dominio
        y el tipo del codominio. En el caso del primero, las funciones son declaradas
        con el tipo del argumento, por lo que se conoce de antemano. Por otro lado,
        para el codominio se debe inferir el tipo extendiendo el ambiente de tipos
        con el argumento de la función.

        La aplicación de una función tiene el tipo del codominio si el argumento que
        se está entregando es del tipo indicado en la declaración de la función.

    Cuando las reglas anteriores no se cumplen, la función debe arrojar errores, para
    ello, siga estos ejemplos:
    >>> (check-type (add (tt) (num 50)) empty-tenv)
    check-type: Addition expects 2 numbers
    >>> (check-type (mul (num 10) (ff)) empty-tenv)
    check-type: Multiplication expects 2 numbers
    >>> (check-type (lt (num 25) (ff)) empty-tenv)
    check-type: Less-than expects 2 numbers
    >>> (check-type ( ite (num 100) (tt) (ff )) empty-tenv)
    check-type: If condition must be a boolean
    >>> (check-type ( ite (tt) (num 500) (ff)) empty-tenv)

        check-type: If branches type mismatch
        >>> (check-type (app (num 100) (num 300)) empty-tenv)
        check-type: Function application to a non-function
        >>> (check-type (app (fun 'x (numT) (mul (id x) (num 2))) (ff)) empty-tenv)
        check-type: Function argument type mismatch

    (e) [7 Pt] Escriba la función
        ;; interp ::   Expr Env -> Value
        que evalúa un Expr hacia un valor final del lenguaje. Asuma que el programa
        (Expr) está bien tipado.

    (f) [3 Pt] Implemente la función
        ;; run ::   <s-expr> -> Value
        que reduce programas en sintaxis concreta a valores del lenguaje, si y solo si, el
        programa está bien tipado.
        Hint: Le puede ser de utilidad la función begin de Racket.
Ejercicio 2                                                                          30 Pt

    (a) [6 Pt] En el mismo archivo (T3.rkt) se define el tipo de datos Prog como un
        lenguaje básico con números, adición, un constructor de pares (conz) y el constructor nil . Implemente la función
        ;; parser ::   <s-prog> -> Prog
        e incluya azúcar sintáctico para definir listas a partir de los constructores conz
        y nil . Puede seguir los ejemplos:
        >>> (parser '( nil ))
        ( nil )
        >>> (parser '(cons 1 2))
        (conz (numbr 1) (numbr 2))
        >>> (parser '(list 1 2 3))
        (conz (numbr 1) (conz (numbr 2) (conz (numbr 3) (nil))))

    (b) [4 Pt] Como se va a implementar pattern matching, se define el tipo de datos
        Pattern. Escriba la función
        ;; parse-pattern ::   <s-prog> -> Pattern
        siguiendo los ejemplos:
        >>> (parse-pattern '3)
        (numP 3)
        >>> (parse-pattern 'x)
        (varP 'x)
        >>> (parse-pattern '( nil ))
        (nilP)
        >>> (parse-pattern '(cons 1 2))

    (conzP (numP 1) (numP 2))
    >>> (parse-pattern '(list 1 2 3))
    (conzP (numP 1) (conzP (numP 2) (conzP (numP 3) (nilP))))

(c) [6 Pt] El desafío de implementar un lenguaje con pattern matching es determinar
    si un valor calza con un patrón dado, encontrando además la asociación entre
    variable y valor. Por ejemplo, el valor (cons (1 (cons 2 3))) calza con el patrón
    (cons x y), en donde x se asocia con 1 e y con (cons 2 3). Por otro lado, el valor
    (cons 2 3) no calza con el patrón (cons x ( nil )) , pues 2 no corresponde con el
    patrón ( nil ).
    En T3.rkt se define el tipo de datos Result que permite retornar un resultado
    exitoso o un mensaje de error sin la necesidad de arrojar una excepción. De esta
    manera, se puede hacer un manejo explícito de los errores. Por ejemplo, se puede
    definir una “división segura”:
    ;; safe-div :: Number Number -> (Result String Number)
    ( define ( safe-div x y)
       (if (zero? y) ( failure "DivisionByZero") (success (/ x y))))
    Bajo esta idea, implemente la función
    ;; generate-substs :: Pattern PValue -> (Result String (Listof (Symbol *
         PValue)))
    que a partir de un patrón y un valor, retorna una lista de substituciones inducidas
    por el pattern matching o un mensaje de error si el match no es exitoso. Para
    ello, asuma que:

        nil es un valor (nilV)

        Un par construido con conz es un valor (conzV) solo si sus componentes son
        valores.

        En un patrón nunca se repite el nombre de una variable.

    >>> (generate-substs (numP 3) (numerical 3))
    (success '())
    >>> (generate-substs (numP 3) (numerical 4))
    ( failure "MatchError: given number does not match pattern")
    >>> (generate-substs (varP 'x) (numerical 3))
    (success (list (cons 'x (numV 3))))
    >>> (generate-substs (numP 3) (nilV))
    ( failure "MatchError: expected a number")
    >>> (generate-substs (nilP) (numerical 45))
    ( failure "MatchError: expected nil")
    >>> (generate-substs (conzP (varP 'x) (nilP)) (numerical 22))
    ( failure "MatchError: expected a cons constructor")

(d) [6 Pt] Escriba la función

    ;; reduce :: Prog (Listof (Symbol * PValue)) -> PValue
   recordando las características de los valores (PValue) mencionados en la sección
   anterior.

(e) [6 Pt] Extienda la gramática BNF del lenguaje, el tipo Prog, la función parser y
    la función reduce para que soporten la expresión match. Siga los ejemplos:
   >>> (parser '(match 2 [1 1] [x 3]))
   (pmatch (numbr 2) (list (cons (numP 1) (numbr 1)) (cons (varP 'x) (numbr 3))))
   >>> (parser '(match (list 1 2 3)))
   SyntaxError: match expression must have at least one case
   >>> (reduce (pmatch (numbr 2) (list (cons (numP 1) (numbr 1)) (cons (varP 'x) (
       numbr 3)))) '())
   (numerical 3)
   >>> (reduce (pmatch (nil) (list (cons (numP 1) (numbr 10)) (cons (numP 2) (
       numbr 20)))) '())
   MatchError: expression does not match any pattern

(f) [2 Pt] ¿Por qué es útil que generate-substs no arroje una excepción sino que
    retorne un mensaje de error?

content.text:
**Evaluating programming approach**

I'm inferring that "play" might relate to some university tasks, potentially involving `test` or `check-expect` from plait. Given that we only need T3.rkt, I could ignore tests if they're unused. I should check for any syntax issues while inspecting the top lines and line endings of T3.rkt. My plan involves implementing all missing functions along with helper definitions, and I might need a parser of s-expressions using `cond` and `match`. I'll also consider defining `parse-list` as a helper for list syntax.

**Evaluating type checking approach**

I should typecheck the function body by extending the environment with argument bindings to their annotated types and inferring the codomain. I'll return an arrow type of the argument type and the body type. For identifiers, I'll lookup their definitions. When it comes to arithmetic and comparisons, I’ll ensure that operands are numeric, and for if statements, the condition needs to be boolean while branches should be of the same type. 

I need to consider whether we should support parsing `true` and `false` as syntax, since the prompt specifies abstract boolean values as `tt` and `ff`, but examples indicate that concrete types could use both.

**Considering boolean symbols**

I might also accept symbols like `tt` and `ff`, but I'm worried this could conflict with identifiers. In the concrete syntax, they specify using `true` and `false`, so I'll map booleans accordingly. It seems like the parser should treat any non-reserved symbols as identifiers. I should keep in mind that using `tt` and `ff` as symbols could lead to confusion, as users likely wouldn't use them as variable names. This could impact how I design it.

**Defining recursive functions**

I’m thinking about defining a recursive function called `same-type?` that uses the match function. That seems like a better approach! I could also implement small helper functions for type comparison. Maybe the `same-type?` function should return a boolean indicating structural equality. It’s all about improving the clarity and efficiency of the code! I just want to make sure I'm heading in the right direction with this idea.

**Clarifying boolean symbols**

I'm pondering the task that uses the terms `true` and `false`. While examples might use `tt` and `ff` abstractly, it seems that in the quoted input, `true` and `false` should be treated as symbols. This means the parser must recognize them as booleans, not as actual Racket booleans. It's a good catch, since tokens in the quoted S-expression are symbols, which adds an interesting layer to how the input is interpreted!

**Evaluating parsing logic**

I need to parse `list` sugar in the parser, ensuring the pattern can accept an empty list as nil or nilP, since a list could have no elements. The case `[(list 'list) (nil)]` seems solid. However, I'm spotting a potential issue when dealing with a 2-element list where the first element might be a reserved symbol — what happens if it’s a symbol? But I think the reserved forms are handled earlier, so that should be okay.

**Considering file requirements**

I realize the current file is mostly placeholder comments, so maybe I don't need huge descriptions. The task states, "Every function must be accompanied by its signature, a brief colloquial description (in T3.rkt), and a significant set of tests (in test.rkt)." I should focus on creating a clear signature, concise descriptions, and effective testing instead. It’s all about ensuring clarity and functionality while following the guidelines laid out in the task!

**Evaluating comments and descriptions**

I’m thinking about the existing comments and realizing they’re mostly insufficient. It seems inserting signature comments and short descriptions for each function could be beneficial. I could format these with ";; parse-type :: ... ; Convierte..." above definitions. While datatypes have BNF comments already, maybe adding short description lines would be helpful, just in case grading overlooks them. Adding brief descriptions for functions and possibly adding one-line colloquial explanations for any missing datatypes might be a good idea. But I want to avoid major edits.

**Considering testing for functions**

I’m thinking that existing comments might actually be sufficient, especially since the BNF provides brief descriptions. I should focus on functions and consider creating tests in T3_tests.rkt. Although it might not be required, adding sample expressions and possibly a `module+ test` could be useful. I wonder if `check-expect` is available in #lang play. Using `displayln` could work, but tests shouldn’t fail on load. It might be safer to include actual tests if the language supports them, and I'll check for that.

**Planning assertions for testing**

I'm thinking we can implement simple checks with `unless (equal? actual expected) (error ...)`. Since the file requires T3.rkt, that approach should work fine without needing a special test framework. I can augment T3_tests.rkt with several assertions, along with adding helper assertions and tests to meet the requirement for "significant tests." I also need to confirm if it's alright to evaluate functions on load and ensure that the T3.rkt file has no syntax errors from misplaced comments or unclosed parentheses.

**Considering file rewrites**

I’m thinking that rewriting the whole T3.rkt file might be easier than making precise edits since it’s a moderate size. With only three files, rewriting T3.rkt and T3_tests.rkt completely could simplify the process. I'll be careful to preserve comments and ensure the syntax is correct. I wonder if `deftype` constructors can be mutually recursive, as some fields refer to other types. I might need to define `Prog` with some match cases and code in Racket style with `match`. There might be an issue with using `empty?` not being imported.

**Exploring Racket functions**

I’m considering the use of `null?` and thinking it might be helpful to use `match` on lists. For example, I can define a function like this: 

```racket
(define (parse-list-expr xs)
  (match xs
    ['() (nil)]
    [(cons h t) (conz (parse h) (parse-list-expr t))]))
```

I need to define `parse` later, so I wonder if mutual recursion is okay. In Racket, it seems I can reference functions defined later in the same module, since they're available when called. So, I can also define `parse-pattern-list` after `parse-pattern`.

**Creating helper asserts**

Alright, I'm considering creating asserts with a helper macro for my code. Since I'm using `#lang play`, I might try to implement `define` and `unless`. I'll start by creating a file and including the relevant requirements. Then, I’ll define a function called `assert-equal`, which will check if the actual result matches the expected outcome. If they don't match, I’ll throw an error with a helpful message. It seems like a solid plan!

**Examining testing output**

I'm thinking about using `displayln` to show "All tests passed." However, I'm unsure if `equal?` will work on structs, so I might want to use `match` instead. Since this file is for manual testing, it's okay, but I need to confirm if the autograder can handle it. I should also clarify the instructions for running tests, possibly using `racket T3_tests.rkt`. I’ll ensure to write the T3.rkt file carefully while checking the availability of `displayln`.

arguments.content:
#lang play

#|
Nombre: 
¿Utilizó Whiteboard Policy? (SI o NO):
En caso afirmativo, ¿con quién?:
¿en qué ejercicio(s)?:
|#


;;------------ ;;
;;==== P1 ==== ;;
;;------------ ;;

#| Parte A |#

#|
<s-type> ::= 'Number
           | 'Boolean
           | (list -> <s-type> <s-type>)
|#

#|
<type> ::= (numT)
         | (boolT)
         | (arrowT <type> <type>)
|#
(deftype Type
  (numT)
  (boolT)
  (arrowT arg ret))

;; parse-type :: <s-type> -> Type
;; Convierte un tipo escrito como dato de Racket al datatype Type.
(define (parse-type st)
  (match st
    ['Number (numT)]
    ['Boolean (boolT)]
    [(list '-> dom cod)
     (arrowT (parse-type dom) (parse-type cod))]
    [else (error 'parse-type "invalid type ~a" st)]))

(define (same-type? t1 t2)
  (match t1
    [(numT) (match t2 [(numT) #t] [_ #f])]
    [(boolT) (match t2 [(boolT) #t] [_ #f])]
    [(arrowT a1 r1)
     (match t2
       [(arrowT a2 r2) (and (same-type? a1 a2) (same-type? r1 r2))]
       [_ #f])]))


#| Parte B |#

#|
<s-expr> ::= <number>
           | true
           | false
           | <symbol>
           | (list + <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (list * <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (list < <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (list if <s-expr> <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (list fun <symbol> <s-type> <s-expr>)
           | (<s-expr> <s-expr>)
|#

#|
<expr> ::= (num <number>)
         | (tt)
         | (ff)
         | (id <symbol>)
         | (add <expr> <expr>)
         | (mul <expr> <expr>)
         | (lt <expr> <expr>)
         | (ite <expr> <expr> <expr>)
         | (fun <symbol> <type> <expr>)
         | (app <expr> <expr>)
|#
(deftype Expr
  (num n)
  (tt)
  (ff)
  (id x)
  (add left right)
  (mul left right)
  (lt left right)
  (ite cond true false)
  (fun arg argtype body)
  (app f arg))

#| Parte C |#

;; parse :: <s-expr> -> Expr
;; Traduce un programa en sintaxis concreta al AST del lenguaje.
(define (parse-list-expr xs)
  (match xs
    ['() (nil)]
    [(cons h t) (conz (parse h) (parse-list-expr t))]))

(define (parse s)
  (match s
    [(? number? n) (num n)]
    ['true (tt)]
    ['false (ff)]
    [(? symbol? x) (id x)]
    [(list '+ l r) (add (parse l) (parse r))]
    [(list '* l r) (mul (parse l) (parse r))]
    [(list '< l r) (lt (parse l) (parse r))]
    [(list 'if c t f) (ite (parse c) (parse t) (parse f))]
    [(list 'fun (? symbol? x) ty body)
     (fun x (parse-type ty) (parse body))]
    [(list f a) (app (parse f) (parse a))]
    [else (error 'parse "invalid expression ~a" s)]))


#| Parte D |#

#| TypeEnv Utils |#
(deftype TypeEnv
  (mtTenv)
  (xTenv id type env))

(define empty-tenv (mtTenv))

(define extend-tenv xTenv)

(define (tenv-lookup x env)
  (match env
    [(mtTenv) (error 'tenv-lookup "free identifier: ~a" x)]
    [(xTenv id type rest) (if (symbol=? x id) type (tenv-lookup x rest))]))

;; check-type :: Expr TypeEnv -> Type
;; Verifica que una expresión esté bien tipada y devuelve su tipo.
(define (check-type e env)
  (match e
    [(num _) (numT)]
    [(tt) (boolT)]
    [(ff) (boolT)]
    [(id x) (tenv-lookup x env)]
    [(add l r)
     (let ([tl (check-type l env)]
           [tr (check-type r env)])
       (if (and (same-type? tl (numT)) (same-type? tr (numT)))
           (numT)
           (error 'check-type "Addition expects 2 numbers")))]
    [(mul l r)
     (let ([tl (check-type l env)]
           [tr (check-type r env)])
       (if (and (same-type? tl (numT)) (same-type? tr (numT)))
           (numT)
           (error 'check-type "Multiplication expects 2 numbers")))]
    [(lt l r)
     (let ([tl (check-type l env)]
           [tr (check-type r env)])
       (if (and (same-type? tl (numT)) (same-type? tr (numT)))
           (boolT)
           (error 'check-type "Less-than expects 2 numbers")))]
    [(ite c t f)
     (let ([tc (check-type c env)]
           [ttype (check-type t env)]
           [ftype (check-type f env)])
       (cond
         [(not (same-type? tc (boolT)))
          (error 'check-type "If condition must be a boolean")]
         [(same-type? ttype ftype) ttype]
         [else (error 'check-type "If branches type mismatch")]))]
    [(fun x ty body)
     (arrowT ty (check-type body (extend-tenv x ty env)))]
    [(app f a)
     (let ([tf (check-type f env)]
           [ta (check-type a env)])
       (match tf
         [(arrowT dom cod)
          (if (same-type? dom ta)
              cod
              (error 'check-type "Function argument type mismatch"))]
         [_ (error 'check-type "Function application to a non-function")]))]))


#| Parte E |#

#| Language Values |#
;; numV y boolV son wrappers de los valores de Racket.
(deftype Value
  (numV n)
  (boolV v)
  (closureV arg body env))

#| Env Utils |#
;; Recuerde que los ambientes guardan valores, no expresiones.
(deftype Env
  (mtEnv)
  (xtEnv id val env))

(define empty-env (mtEnv))

(define extend-env xtEnv)

(define (lookup x env)
  (match env
    [(mtEnv) (error 'lookup "free identifier: ~a" x)]
    [(xtEnv id val rest) (if (symbol=? x id) val (lookup x rest))]))

;; interp :: Expr Env -> Value
;; Evalúa una expresión bien tipada usando ambientes de valores.
(define (interp e env)
  (match e
    [(num n) (numV n)]
    [(tt) (boolV #t)]
    [(ff) (boolV #f)]
    [(id x) (lookup x env)]
    [(add l r)
     (let ([vl (interp l env)]
           [vr (interp r env)])
       (match vl
         [(numV n) (match vr [(numV m) (numV (+ n m))] [_ (error 'interp "addition expects numbers")])]
         [_ (error 'interp "addition expects numbers")]))]
    [(mul l r)
     (let ([vl (interp l env)]
           [vr (interp r env)])
       (match vl
         [(numV n) (match vr [(numV m) (numV (* n m))] [_ (error 'interp "multiplication expects numbers")])]
         [_ (error 'interp "multiplication expects numbers")]))]
    [(lt l r)
     (let ([vl (interp l env)]
           [vr (interp r env)])
       (match vl
         [(numV n) (match vr [(numV m) (boolV (< n m))] [_ (error 'interp "less-than expects numbers")])]
         [_ (error 'interp "less-than expects numbers")]))]
    [(ite c t f)
     (match (interp c env)
       [(boolV #t) (interp t env)]
       [(boolV #f) (interp f env)]
       [_ (error 'interp "if condition must be boolean")])]
    [(fun x ty body) (closureV x body env)]
    [(app f a)
     (match (interp f env)
       [(closureV x body fenv)
        (interp body (extend-env x (interp a env) fenv))]
       [_ (error 'interp "application to non-function")])]))


#| Parte F |#

;; run :: <s-expr> -> Value
;; Parsea, typechequea e interpreta un programa completo.
(define (run s)
  (let ([e (parse s)])
    (begin
      (check-type e empty-tenv)
      (interp e empty-env))))


;;------------ ;;
;;==== P2 ==== ;;
;;------------ ;;

#| Parte A y E |#

#|
<prog> ::= (numbr <number>)
         | (variable <symbol>)
         | (plus <prog> <prog>)
         | (nil)
         | (conz <prog> <prog>)
         | (pmatch <prog> <case> ...)
         | ...

<case> ::= (cons <pattern> <prog>)
|#
(deftype Prog
  (numbr n)
  (plus l r)
  (nil)
  (conz a b)
  (variable x)
  (pmatch expr cases))

#|
<s-expr> ::= <number>
           | <symbol>
           | (list + <s-prog> <s-prog>)
           | (list nil)
           | (list cons <s-prog> <s-prog>)
           | (list list <s-prog> ...)
           | (list match <s-prog> [<pattern> <s-prog>] ...)
           | ...
|#

;; parser :: <s-prog> -> Prog
;; Traduce la sintaxis concreta del lenguaje de programas.
(define (parse-list-prog xs)
  (match xs
    ['() (nil)]
    [(cons h t) (conz (parser h) (parse-list-prog t))]))

(define (parse-case c)
  (match c
    [(list p b) (cons (parse-pattern p) (parser b))]
    [else (error 'SyntaxError "malformed match case")]))

(define (parse-cases cs)
  (match cs
    ['() '()]
    [(cons c rest) (cons (parse-case c) (parse-cases rest))]))

(define (parser s)
  (match s
    [(? number? n) (numbr n)]
    [(? symbol? x) (variable x)]
    [(list '+ l r) (plus (parser l) (parser r))]
    [(list 'nil) (nil)]
    [(list 'cons a b) (conz (parser a) (parser b))]
    [(list 'list elems ...) (parse-list-prog elems)]
    [(list 'match e cs ...)
     (if (null? cs)
         (error 'SyntaxError "match expression must have at least one case")
         (pmatch (parser e) (parse-cases cs)))]
    [else (error 'SyntaxError "invalid program ~a" s)]))


#| Parte B |#

#|
<pattern> ::= (numP <number>)
            | (varP <symbol>)
            | (nilP)
            | (conzP <pattern> <pattern>)
|#
(deftype Pattern
  (numP n)
  (varP x)
  (nilP)
  (conzP a b))

;; parse-pattern :: <s-prog> -> Pattern
;; Convierte una sintaxis concreta de patrón al datatype Pattern.
(define (parse-pattern p)
  (match p
    [(? number? n) (numP n)]
    [(? symbol? x) (varP x)]
    [(list 'nil) (nilP)]
    [(list 'cons a b) (conzP (parse-pattern a) (parse-pattern b))]
    [(list 'list elems ...) (parse-pattern-list elems)]
    [else (error 'SyntaxError "invalid pattern ~a" p)]))

(define (parse-pattern-list xs)
  (match xs
    ['() (nilP)]
    [(cons h t) (conzP (parse-pattern h) (parse-pattern-list t))]))


#| Parte C |#

#|
<pvalue> ::= (numerical <number>)
          | (nilV)
          | (conzV <value> <value>)
|#
(deftype PValue
  (numerical n)
  (nilV)
  (conzV a b))

#|
<result> e v ::= (failure e)
               | (success v)
|#
(deftype Result
  (failure e)
  (success v))

;; generate-substs :: Pattern PValue -> (Result String (Listof (Symbol * PValue)))
;; Genera las substituciones inducidas por el pattern matching o un error explícito.
(define (generate-substs pat val)
  (match pat
    [(numP n)
     (match val
       [(numerical m)
        (if (= n m)
            (success '())
            (failure "MatchError: given number does not match pattern"))]
       [_ (failure "MatchError: expected a number")])]
    [(varP x) (success (list (cons x val)))]
    [(nilP)
     (match val
       [(nilV) (success '())]
       [_ (failure "MatchError: expected nil")])]
    [(conzP a b)
     (match val
       [(conzV v1 v2)
        (match (generate-substs a v1)
          [(failure e) (failure e)]
          [(success s1)
           (match (generate-substs b v2)
             [(failure e) (failure e)]
             [(success s2) (success (append s1 s2))])])]
       [_ (failure "MatchError: expected a cons constructor")])]))


#| Parte D y E |#

;; num+ PValue PValue -> PValue
(define (num+ v1 v2)
  (match v1
    [(numerical n) (match v2
                [(numerical m) (numerical (+ n m))]
                [_ (error "TypeError: expected a number")])]
    [_ (error "TypeError: expected a number")]))

;; reduce :: Prog (Listof (Symbol * PValue)) -> PValue
;; Reduce un programa usando un ambiente de substituciones.
(define (subst-lookup x env)
  (match env
    ['() (error 'reduce "free identifier: ~a" x)]
    [(cons (cons id val) rest)
     (if (symbol=? x id) val (subst-lookup x rest))]))

(define (reduce-match val cases env)
  (match cases
    ['() (error 'MatchError "expression does not match any pattern")]
    [(cons (cons pat body) rest)
     (match (generate-substs pat val)
       [(success subs) (reduce body (append subs env))]
       [(failure _) (reduce-match val rest env)])]))

(define (reduce p env)
  (match p
    [(numbr n) (numerical n)]
    [(plus l r) (num+ (reduce l env) (reduce r env))]
    [(nil) (nilV)]
    [(conz a b) (conzV (reduce a env) (reduce b env))]
    [(variable x) (subst-lookup x env)]
    [(pmatch e cases) (reduce-match (reduce e env) cases env)]))


#| Parte F |#

#|
Respuesta:
|#

**Assessing function definitions**

I need to compile issues with `parse-list-expr` because it’s defined, but the `parse` function uses it. Wait, `parse-list-expr` shouldn't handle list sugar in Expr. It references `nil` and `conz`, which are defined later. Since `parse-list-expr` is unused, it’s not causing issues now, but having undefined names may be okay until needed. I think `match` patterns with symbols `tt` and `ff` could work too, and everything appears fine in the constructor patterns.

**Identifying potential issues in parsing**

I’m spotting a possible syntax issue in the `parse` generic where `(list f a)` matches any two-list. However, reserved forms already matched earlier, which is a relief. There’s a concern with `parse-list-prog` and `parse-list-expr` referencing `conz` and `nil`, which are defined later. But since Racket allows references to later definitions within a module as it compiles as a whole, that should be fine. Also, `parse-list-expr` isn’t used anyway. However, in `check-type`, `ite` evaluates both branches even if the condition isn’t Boolean, which could lead to errors.

**Evaluating branch handling in check-type**

I’m considering that if one branch has a free identifier, `check-type` might throw an error before evaluating the condition since it computes both branches eagerly. The spec probably doesn't care, but it could be better to check the condition first to avoid unnecessary errors. Should I adjust this? I’m thinking it makes sense to align the expected errors so that a branch mismatch only occurs if the condition type is valid and if the condition is invalid, the focus should be on that error.

**Evaluating type checks**

I’m considering the error handling approach. It seems like checking conditions before branches is important to avoid ill-typed inputs. I think evaluating function types and argument types is also necessary. If the function type is invalid, errors might arise from the argument type instead. To maintain error precedence, it might be best to compute the condition first. If there's a boolean condition, I should check branches afterward. Let's make that adjustment to the `check-type` function!

**Improving type checks**

I'm thinking about the `add/mul/lt` functions. If the left operand isn't numeric while the right one has a free identifier, it may cause confusion in error reporting. So I should check the left operand first, and throw an error immediately if it's not numeric. Then I'll check the right operand afterward to align error messages. I’m also considering how the `match` function behaves with empty cases; I believe it can match even zero elements, so a check for `null` should work properly. Let’s make these adjustments!

**Clarifying parser behavior**

I need to consider a `parser` for `match` to handle cases that aren't exactly two elements long, which could lead to parse-case errors. It's also important to create a special case for the bare symbol `nil`. Currently, the `parse-pattern` treats any symbol as a variable, including `nil`. When handling input like `(nil)`, it matches it as `(list 'nil)`, which is something I should keep in mind. For symbol `nil`, it should be treated as a variable, but I have to ensure clarity in the match examples. So, let's refine this approach!

**Analyzing Racket compilation**

I'm wondering if the module compile can handle forward references in top-level define values. In Racket, references to later definitions are usually fine because the module compiler handles it. But if there's an expression being evaluated at compile time, it might get tricky. Using `match` patterns with `? number?` and `? symbol?` seems likely, but I could also go with `number?` in a cond. I think checking the file for syntax issues and fixing any compile errors is a good next step. Maybe I'll run `racket T3_tests.rkt`, but it might fail since the tests aren't written yet, so I should compile T3.rkt first.

**Considering test expansions**

The task is about creating and expanding the T3_tests.rkt with significant tests. Since there's no test harness, I'll rely on a simple assertion function. I'll need to check equality for values of custom datatypes, possibly with `equal?` for structures from deftype. I should also consider testing error raising, potentially using `with-handlers`. It makes sense to implement a helper like `assert-error` for error message verification. However, it might get a bit complicated, so I'll focus on covering success paths while including some error tests.

**Drafting test functions**

I’m considering creating a file with the following code:

```
#lang play
(require "T3.rkt")

(define (assert-equal actual expected)
  (unless (equal? actual expected) (error 'test ...)))

(define (assert-error thunk expected-msg)
  (with-handlers ([exn:fail? (lambda (e) (unless (equal? (exn-message e) expected-msg) (error ...)))])
    (thunk)
    (error 'test "expected error")))
```

I need to confirm that `exn-message` is available in Racket. It seems essential for handling errors properly. Let’s ensure everything functions as intended!

arguments.content:
#lang play

(require "T3.rkt")

(define (assert-equal actual expected name)
  (unless (equal? actual expected)
    (error 'tests "~a: expected ~a, got ~a" name expected actual)))

(define (assert-error thunk expected name)
  (with-handlers ([exn:fail?
                   (lambda (e)
                     (unless (equal? (exn-message e) expected)
                       (error 'tests "~a: expected error ~a, got ~a"
                              name expected (exn-message e))))])
    (thunk)
    (error 'tests "~a: expected error ~a, got success" name expected)))

;; P1
(assert-equal (parse-type 'Number) (numT) 'parse-type-number)
(assert-equal (parse-type '(-> Number Boolean)) (arrowT (numT) (boolT)) 'parse-type-arrow)
(assert-equal (parse '(fun x Number (+ x 1)))
              (fun 'x (numT) (add (id 'x) (num 1)))
              'parse-fun)
(assert-equal (parse '((fun x Number (* x 2)) 10))
              (app (fun 'x (numT) (mul (id 'x) (num 2))) (num 10))
              'parse-app)
(assert-equal (check-type (parse '(fun x Number (+ x 1))) empty-tenv)
              (arrowT (numT) (numT))
              'check-type-fun)
(assert-equal (run '((fun x Number (+ x 1)) 10)) (numV 11) 'run-app)
(assert-error (lambda () (check-type (add (tt) (num 50)) empty-tenv))
              "check-type: Addition expects 2 numbers"
              'check-type-add-error)

;; P2
(assert-equal (parser '(nil)) (nil) 'parser-nil)
(assert-equal (parser '(cons 1 2)) (conz (numbr 1) (numbr 2)) 'parser-cons)
(assert-equal (parser '(list 1 2 3))
              (conz (numbr 1) (conz (numbr 2) (conz (numbr 3) (nil))))
              'parser-list)
(assert-equal (parser '(match 2 [1 1] [x 3]))
              (pmatch (numbr 2)
                      (list (cons (numP 1) (numbr 1))
                            (cons (varP 'x) (numbr 3))))
              'parser-match)
(assert-equal (parse-pattern '(list 1 2 3))
              (conzP (numP 1) (conzP (numP 2) (conzP (numP 3) (nilP))))
              'parse-pattern-list)
(assert-equal (generate-substs (varP 'x) (numerical 3))
              (success (list (cons 'x (numerical 3))))
              'generate-substs-var)
(assert-equal (reduce (pmatch (numbr 2)
                              (list (cons (numP 1) (numbr 1))
                                    (cons (varP 'x) (numbr 3))))
                      '())
              (numerical 3)
              'reduce-match)
(assert-error (lambda () (parser '(match (list 1 2 3))))
              "SyntaxError: match expression must have at least one case"
              'parser-match-empty)

(displayln "All T3 tests passed")
